review-details_isCollapsed__Bsg_9: None but money and reputation
review-details_isCollapsed__Bsg_9 2: When I first started working at Asian Paints, I was overjoyed. The company's great reputation, the promises of a bright career, and the feeling of being part of such a prestigious organization had me on cloud nine. Little did I know that my dream job would soon become a grueling nightmare. The main problem here is the excessive demands on TSOs, the sales force's front-line workers. We're expected to work long hours, from 10 AM to an exhausting 12 AM, getting home or to our small rooms at 1 AM, and doing this 15 days a month. This schedule is not just tiring; it's inhumane. The term "peak" at Asian Paints has nothing to do with reaching the summit; instead, it's about pushing us to our limits. During these peak times, managers seem to enjoy calling us in and subjecting us to harsh criticism and long talks about sales numbers. The pressure is too much, and it's common for TSOs to develop persistent headaches due to the constant stress. This working culture is not just wrong; it's flat out against the law. No one should have to endure such harsh conditions, where 15-16 hour workdays are considered standard. It's sad to see that many employees have become so used to this tough environment that they've chosen to accept it as the cost of making more money. They've traded their health and well-being for a bigger paycheck. The most disappointing part is that many employees have become complacent. Long-time workers, who should know better, stay silent in the face of this modern-day form of slavery because they're comfortable with their income. It's the new recruits and those new to the industry who dare to speak up, realizing the importance of their health and a balance between work and life. The job of a TSO is not just one job; it's a combination of responsibilities. Besides reaching sales goals, we're also expected to handle product shipments, customer complaints, issues with dealers, matching purchase orders, customer visits, and even selling products from different categories like adhesives and smartcare. Additionally, we're tasked with installing machines, collecting payments, and relentlessly chasing overdue payments. This is not a job; it's a sentence to a living nightmare. Nobody should have to go through what Asian Paints puts its TSOs through. It's high time that a company as prestigious as this reevaluates its work culture and policies for employee welfare before it damages its reputation further and loses the trust of potential employees. This situation is a stark reminder that a company's name and reputation should not come at the cost of its employees' well-being.

The general perception is since the manager has been duly evaluated and promoted as a Senior manager he ca not fail. Responsibility of all failures is with the managers

+Six-day weeks in the field sales organization. At least 4-5 days per month when one has to stay in office till midnight. Affect work-life balance.
+Strategy, HR and Systems fully centralized in Bombay/Mumbai. Does not have pulse of field staff spread all over the country. A sense of decisions being taken at the top and ruthlessly rolled out nationwide. Efficient, but disengages middle and lower management and staff.

review-details_detailsLink__NxIv5: Great perks, very talented people and horrible work-life balance
review-details_isCollapsed__Bsg_9:
- Salary bracket for managers is very high. One of the best pay masters in the industry for manager level roles.
- Excellent colleagues. Best and brightest of the country come here to work.
- Largely meritocratic culture. At-least in manufacturing locations. Not sure about corporate roles.
- Good perks and benefits. After 5 years you can get a year off sabbatical ,provided it is approved by your manager. I don't know any other company that offers such a policy. At-least not a year off may be a few months.
review-details_isCollapsed__Bsg_9 2:
- Absolutely horrendous work life balance. So bad that no one in top management even talks about it.
- Stress. A lot of stress. Be ready to have serious health issues caused by stress of working long hours
- There is a culture of perfection with associated ruthlessness. Top managers would push you to any extent to achieve targets. And be mindful all the targets set are extremely demanding. Like 50% improvement in everything. Expectations are just so very high. It is as if Elon Musk was running a paint company.
- Mental health is given zero importance.
- Growth is very very limited. You compete with absolute best and then it takes at-least 5 years to grow from one level to another (You need to be absolute best, otherwise wait for 7-8 years or even never).
- Salaries as you go up aren't as great.
- HRs are powerless in this organization.
- Extremely conservative. management lacks bold decision making.
